video_title: Think Like Everyone Else, Lose Like Everyone Else | Brent Donnelly
gist: FX trader Brent Donnelly (Spectrum Markets) argues that a trader's durable edge in 2026 is predicting human/consensus behavior rather than central-bank policy, and applies it to markets: he expects new Fed chair Kevin Warsh's hawkishness to fade into dovishness (barring a strong payrolls print), sees a broad-dollar/rate-differential regime with the 'debasement trade' having capitulated (bearish gold/yen-strength trades), and views Bitcoin as having run out of new narratives. He also promotes his book 'Trade Outside the Box' on poker, addiction psychology, ergodicity, and using LLMs as a consensus-detector.

insights.claim: Brent Donnelly's core investing thesis is that traders need divergent or independent thinking to have an edge because most people underperform the index and most traders lose money, so thinking like everyone else guarantees average-to-poor performance.
insights.speaker: guest, Brent Donnelly (Spectrum Markets president)
insights.so_what: Frames the entire episode: an actionable edge comes from importing non-market mental models and fading/leaning consensus, not from consuming more mainstream research.
insights.quote: if you think like everyone else you'll perform like everyone else

insights.claim: Donnelly says his edge has shifted over 15 years from predicting macro policy (ECB hikes, data) to predicting where humans/positioning will go over the next week or two, because humans are easier to predict than macroeconomic policy or data.
insights.speaker: guest, Brent Donnelly
insights.so_what: Signals a behavioral/positioning-driven, short-horizon trading style; useful lens for interpreting his specific FX and Fed calls as flow/sentiment bets rather than fundamental forecasts.
insights.quote: I'm so much less trying to predict like what the central bank's going to do or what the data is going to do and I'm more trying to predict like what are the humans going to do

insights.claim: Donnelly invokes Keynes's beauty-contest framework and a Jim Grant maxim that the key to making money is thinking what everyone else thinks but slightly before them, as the operating principle behind narrative-based trading.
insights.speaker: guest, Brent Donnelly
insights.so_what: Reframes narrative analysis as front-running consensus formation, not being contrarian for its own sake.
insights.quote: you're trying to pick who everyone else will think is most beautiful

insights.claim: Donnelly's personal failure mode is over-trading when he is over-earning (taking more risk after big wins) rather than chasing losses, and he counters it mechanically with conditional formatting in his P&L spreadsheet that forces him to cut line items and risk exactly when instincts say to double down.
insights.speaker: guest, Brent Donnelly
insights.so_what: Concrete, copyable risk-management tactic: build automated triggers that reduce size after a P&L spike, the point of maximum mean-reversion risk.
insights.quote: when I'm over earning I'm reducing the number of line items and reducing my risk at the exact moment that my instincts are all like let's double down

insights.claim: Donnelly argues that even elite traders who run strategies that are extremely convex in the wrong direction are statistically bound to blow up every five to six years, citing Jesse Livermore and Victor Niederhoffer as gifted traders undone by ergodicity/risk-of-ruin.
insights.speaker: guest, Brent Donnelly
insights.so_what: Argues for permanently avoiding strategies with negative-convexity tail exposure regardless of skill; a filter for evaluating any manager's return profile.
insights.quote: if you step close enough to the abyss enough times you're going to slip in a couple times

insights.claim: Donnelly notes that empirical research shows incremental information past a surprisingly low threshold makes traders more confident but less accurate, which he warns is acute in 2026 given information overload.
insights.speaker: guest, Brent Donnelly
insights.so_what: Cautions against LLM-fueled over-research; more inputs can degrade decision quality, an argument for constrained information diets.
insights.quote: incremental information makes you more confident and less accurate

insights.claim: Donnelly frames the danger of screen-based intraday trading through 'Addiction by Design,' describing a 'dark zone' where the reactive monkey mind overrides System 2, drawing an explicit parallel between slot-machine feedback loops and hyperactive trading.
insights.speaker: guest, Brent Donnelly
insights.so_what: Behavioral risk lens: treat compulsive intraday activity (five-minute-candle X culture) as a gambling-disorder analog to be structurally curbed, not willpowered away.

insights.claim: Donnelly uses LLMs primarily to generate the most vanilla consensus view on a stock or macro question, treating the output as a map of what the sell-side consensus and its key metrics are, not as an original thesis.
insights.speaker: guest, Brent Donnelly
insights.so_what: Practical edge: use LLMs to rapidly benchmark consensus, then trade divergences between that consensus and current price.
insights.quote: what an LLM is good for is like seeing okay what are the what's the consensus

insights.claim: Donnelly's LLM playbook for headlines is to run three LLMs at once on a breaking story in an unfamiliar sector, and if they converge on a bearish read (e.g., a biotech FDA setback implying a ~50% hit) while the stock is only down ~9% three minutes after the headline, short it with a stop at the news level targeting a 30% decline.
insights.speaker: guest, Brent Donnelly
insights.so_what: A repeatable event-driven tactic: exploit the lag between an LLM-detectable consensus and price that hasn't yet caught up.
insights.quote: I usually use three LLMs just all at once to make sure one of them is not on mushrooms or whatever

insights.claim: Donnelly feeds time series into Claude and asks it to find tradable patterns, treating the output as flawed data-mining but useful for idea generation when a domain expert can filter obvious junk from a genuinely novel flow insight.
insights.speaker: guest, Brent Donnelly
insights.so_what: Reframes LLM data-snooping as a hypothesis generator (to be validated), not a systematic strategy that would fail out of sample.
insights.quote: find any interesting patterns in this that might be tradable

insights.claim: On the reflexivity of mass LLM usage, Donnelly agrees that when a hot theme emerges, the trade is often to lean into momentum by buying the names LLMs will name as top picks, because that is the consensus everyone else will buy before it is fully priced.
insights.speaker: guest, Brent Donnelly
insights.so_what: Second-order LLM trade: for themes on the cusp of mainstream (not yet in the zeitgeist), buy the LLM-obvious basket; skip themes like memory that are already fully priced.
insights.quote: tell me the best three humanoid robotics stocks. Probably those are the ones you want to buy because that's what everyone else is going to buy

insights.claim: Donnelly compares reading LLM consensus to watching swaggystocks.com / r/WallStreetBets flow, using timing as the signal: buy a name freshly appearing (e.g., Wendy's) before the crowd bores of it, but fade one that has sat atop the rankings (e.g., GLD for three weeks with a parabolic options chart) as a sign consensus is fully in the price.
insights.speaker: guest, Brent Donnelly
insights.so_what: Sentiment-timing rule: novelty of a name in crowd-flow data is bullish; persistence at the top is a capitulation/exhaustion tell.

themes.theme: Warsh Fed and the rate path

insights.claim: Donnelly's base case is that new Fed chair Kevin Warsh's initial hawkishness is performative and he will turn materially more dovish over time, reverting to the Fed's perma-forecast that core PCE reaches 2% in two years.
insights.speaker: guest, Brent Donnelly
insights.so_what: If correct, current pricing of hikes overshoots; sets up dovish repricing trades (lower front-end yields, softer dollar) later in 2026.
insights.quote: as a new Fed chair you have to be hawkish you have to say we're committed to the inflation target like that's just what you do

insights.claim: Donnelly says the market swung from pricing a few Fed cuts to a couple of hikes by year-end on the back of the Iran war, but with oil now sub-70 he expects cooling inflation expectations to let the Fed slow-play and leave Fed funds unchanged into December.
insights.speaker: guest, Brent Donnelly
insights.so_what: Implies fading the hawkish curve repricing; a rates-market view that the hikes now priced won't materialize absent strong data.
insights.quote: you'll be you sitting there in December with Fed funds unchanged

insights.claim: Donnelly flags a strong non-farm payrolls print as the key risk to his dovish Warsh call, noting a strategic-hawk cohort believes hiking early (even a July hike or a teed-up September hike if payrolls is strong) lets the Fed hike less later, so he would be nervous around the July FOMC on a hot jobs number.
insights.speaker: guest, Brent Donnelly
insights.so_what: Defines the trigger to flip the trade: watch the payrolls print and, more importantly, the July FOMC reaction to it.
insights.quote: if you hike now you can probably hike less and maybe even cut later

insights.claim: Donnelly is skeptical the end of forward guidance meaningfully changes the FX process, disputing the 'new Greenspan-style regime' narrative and noting Warsh flip-flopped from touting trimmed-mean inflation (dovish) to raging hawkishness while adding five new task forces despite promising a streamlined Fed.
insights.speaker: guest, Brent Donnelly
insights.so_what: Counters the popular 'regime change' framing; the main practical change is data mattering slightly more without inter-meeting guidance smoothing.
insights.quote: people are saying like it's a new regime he's going back to green span style. It just doesn't I don't think that's true

themes.theme: Broad-dollar regime and the debasement trade

insights.claim: Donnelly characterizes current FX as a monolithic broad-dollar trade driven by rate differentials, with previously idiosyncratic pairs like dollar-Korea and dollar-Brazil now tracking euro-dollar, and only Japan remaining a genuine idiosyncratic story.
insights.speaker: guest, Brent Donnelly
insights.so_what: Implies FX positioning should be expressed as a single dollar factor plus a separate Japan bet, rather than many independent cross trades.
insights.quote: even things like dollar Brazil have been rallying

insights.claim: Donnelly notes carry and momentum CTAs plus relative-value bond funds are reinforcing the dollar move because the widened US yield differential makes sitting in dollars more attractive, which has also pressured gold lower.
insights.speaker: guest, Brent Donnelly
insights.so_what: Explains the flow mechanics behind dollar-up/gold-down; watch rate differentials as the leading signal for both.

insights.claim: Donnelly believes the debasement trade has over-subscribed and then capitulated, citing gold options skew, positioning metrics, and nobody trading GLD on WallStreetBets anymore, and thinks the pendulum has swung too far given Warsh likely won't deliver the most hawkish outcome.
insights.speaker: guest, Brent Donnelly
insights.so_what: Sets up a mean-reversion / counter-trend view: the capitulation in debasement assets may be nearly complete, arguing against chasing the dollar/gold-down move much further.
insights.quote: debasement trade is in debasement at the moment

insights.claim: Donnelly reports that dollar-Swiss versus gold and the US-Germany yield spread versus euro-dollar have both been fitting tightly recently, with Swiss the most gold-like currency, confirming a return to a traditional rate-differential FX regime.
insights.speaker: guest, Brent Donnelly
insights.so_what: Gives concrete cross-asset overlays to monitor the regime; a breakdown in these fits would signal the dollar trade is losing its driver.

insights.claim: Donnelly describes dollar-yen as effectively pegged near 162 with heavy jump risk: no one will buy above 162 for fear of MOF intervention, but there are too few sellers, creating a binary where either the Ministry of Finance is absent and it explodes higher or it intervenes and yen jumps ~4%.
insights.speaker: guest, Brent Donnelly
insights.so_what: Frames dollar-yen as a low-vol, high-tail-risk setup; favors options/convexity structures over spot for the intervention event.
insights.quote: it's like a pegged currency with a ton of jump risk

insights.claim: Donnelly argues unilateral MOF intervention doesn't work long-term because no policy variables are aligned (BOJ not hiking aggressively, Fed not cutting, Japan running loose fiscal), so the yen behaves like a beach ball held underwater that keeps bouncing back.
insights.speaker: guest, Brent Donnelly
insights.so_what: Implies short-dollar-yen trades into interventions are quick scalps to exit fast, not durable positions.
insights.quote: it's like a beach ball underwater. You can keep pushing it but it just keeps on bouncing back

insights.claim: Donnelly contends only coordinated intervention (Bessent/Fed participating with a joint statement that the yen is undervalued) has historically produced durable tops, but he does not expect that to happen, so the cat-and-mouse continues.
insights.speaker: guest, Brent Donnelly
insights.so_what: Sets the bar for a real yen reversal; absent coordinated action, expect repeated failed unilateral interventions to be bought.
insights.quote: coordinated intervention has worked almost every time

insights.claim: Donnelly says the durable drivers that would send dollar-yen lower for real are a US recession, lower US two-year yields, or coordinated intervention, none of which he expects to occur in 2026.
insights.speaker: guest, Brent Donnelly
insights.so_what: Defines the fundamental checklist for a lasting yen rally; until one appears, structural yen weakness persists.

insights.claim: Donnelly identifies Japan's GPIF and Kampo pension funds as holding an almost incomprehensibly large, trillions-scale asset pool that could reverse their Abenomics-era foreign allocation, repatriating from US bonds into JGBs as a one-time 'holy grail' stabilizer for both JGBs and the yen.
insights.speaker: guest, Brent Donnelly
insights.so_what: A latent policy tail risk for US Treasuries: a Japanese repatriation shift could pressure US bonds while stabilizing JGBs/yen; a scenario to hedge but likely reserved for emergencies.
insights.quote: it's like a trick you can only do once

insights.claim: Donnelly frames JGB and UK long-end yield scares as velocity/volatility events, not level events, warning of an emerging-markets-style feedback loop where rising yields weaken the currency and holders sell bonds and FX together, which he says Japan has flirted with about three times.
insights.speaker: guest, Brent Donnelly
insights.so_what: Tells investors to monitor the speed of yield moves, not absolute levels, as the trigger for currency-plus-bond crises.
insights.quote: it's a velocity thing not a levels thing

insights.claim: Donnelly argues equities tolerate yields drifting from 2% to 4% over ~19 months but sell off when yields move ~40bps in a week, so bond volatility, not the yield level, is the key equity risk signal (as in 2022).
insights.speaker: guest, Brent Donnelly
insights.so_what: A concrete equity risk trigger: watch weekly rate-move magnitude and MOVE-style bond vol rather than the 10-year level itself.
insights.quote: if yields are, you know, and so you look at the volatility of bonds, if if yields are moving 40 basis points in in a week, then stocks start to get scared

insights.claim: Donnelly views the global rise toward ~4% US yields as a healthy return to pre-2008 'normal capitalism,' and dismisses the long-standing view that high debt makes higher yields impossible, noting US-debt-unsustainability warnings have been made since the 1985 Times Square debt clock.
insights.speaker: guest, Brent Donnelly
insights.so_what: Pushes back on debt-doom positioning; higher-for-longer yields can persist and coexist with functioning markets far longer than bears expect.
insights.quote: it is weirdly sustainable for much longer than

insights.claim: The host argues the reason large government debt hasn't triggered a crisis is that consumer and private debt must be repaid while government debt effectively need not be, with inflation or a higher S&P 500 acting as the release valves for oversized deficits.
insights.speaker: host (Felix)
insights.so_what: Reframes the fiscal-sustainability debate; if inflation and nominal equity gains are the release valves, positioning long nominal/productive assets over hard-money hedges follows.

insights.claim: Donnelly is skeptical of gold as an inflation hedge, saying people over-weight the 1980s example and that empirically it is hard to establish gold hedges inflation consistently, with the past year's gold run arguably explained by Chinese central-bank buying.
insights.speaker: guest, Brent Donnelly
insights.so_what: Undercuts the reflexive 'own gold for inflation' thesis; if the driver is Chinese official buying, gold's case weakens once that flow fades.
insights.quote: it's it's really hard to find whether it is or not. Like sometimes it is, sometimes it isn't

insights.claim: Donnelly argues Bitcoin has exhausted its rotating narratives (store of value, cash replacement, digital gold, risk asset, inflation hedge) with none panning out, leaving it a 'weird idiosyncratic thing' that Michael Sailor buys and sells, and he doubts a new bull-run narrative will emerge for a long time.
insights.speaker: guest, Brent Donnelly
insights.so_what: Bearish/neutral structural view on Bitcoin: without a fresh believable narrative to make new highs, the next major rally may be far off.
insights.quote: you used up all the cool narratives. What like what's next?

insights.claim: The host observes that the assets designed for fiscal recklessness (gold, Bitcoin) have underperformed while equities keep making all-time highs, because investors prefer a productive asset throwing off nominal earnings in a higher-inflation world where everything rises nominally.
insights.speaker: host (Felix)
insights.so_what: Supports overweighting productive nominal-earnings assets over non-yielding hard-money hedges as the debasement-era trade.
insights.quote: people would prefer to own a productive asset that's giving off like nominal earnings in a world where everything's going higher nominally

insights.claim: Donnelly notes the biggest money-making stocks and even Buffett's largest wins (Apple, Geico) were not value stocks, and speculates value investing has lagged because returns are driven by earnings growth that value names lack relative to hyperscalers, though he holds the view loosely.
insights.speaker: guest, Brent Donnelly
insights.so_what: Reinforces growth/earnings-momentum over classic value; treat cheap value names as potential value traps absent an earnings catalyst.
